title: Lesson 002 – Making the Subjunctive Automatic
grammarFocus: keeping the subjunctive · avere vs essere in the past subjunctive · noun vs verb · anche/ancora/già · never dropping 'che'
objective: Make the present and past subjunctive automatic by drilling the five traps that most often break it: reverting to the indicative, choosing the wrong auxiliary, mixing up nouns and verbs, confusing small words, and dropping 'che'.

corePatterns.pattern: Penso / Credo / Mi sembra che + congiuntivo
È importante che / Bisogna che + congiuntivo
è possibile che / può darsi che / sembra che / pare che

sentences.italian: Penso che tu abbia bisogno di riposo.
sentences.literal: I think that you have-subjunctive need of rest.
sentences.natural: I think you need some rest.
sentences.note: Trap: after 'penso che' use the subjunctive 'abbia', NOT the indicative 'hai'. ('avere bisogno di' = to need.)

sentences.italian: Credo che questo metodo funzioni bene.
sentences.literal: I believe that this method work-subjunctive well.
sentences.natural: I think this method works well.

sentences.italian: Mi sembra che lui faccia sul serio.
sentences.literal: It seems to me that he do-subjunctive on serious.
sentences.natural: It seems to me he's serious about it.
sentences.note: Trap: 'faccia' (subjunctive of fare) NOT 'fa' (indicative). 'fare sul serio' = to mean it / be serious.

sentences.italian: È importante che tu sia puntuale.
sentences.literal: It is important that you be-subjunctive punctual.
sentences.natural: It's important that you're on time.
sentences.note: 'È importante che' forces the subjunctive: 'sia', NOT the indicative 'sei'.

sentences.italian: Bisogna che tu lo faccia subito.
sentences.literal: It is necessary that you it do-subjunctive right-away.
sentences.natural: You need to do it right away.
sentences.note: 'Bisogna che' forces the subjunctive: 'tu faccia', NOT the indicative 'tu fai'.

sentences.italian: Penso che abbiano già deciso tutto.
sentences.literal: I think that they have-subjunctive already decided everything.
sentences.natural: I think they've already decided everything.
sentences.note: 'decidere' takes AVERE → 'abbiano deciso', NOT 'siano deciso'. With avere the participle does not agree. ('già' = already.)

sentences.italian: Credo che lei abbia finito il lavoro.
sentences.literal: I believe that she have-subjunctive finished the work.
sentences.natural: I think she's finished the work.
sentences.note: 'finire' (with an object) takes AVERE → 'abbia finito'. Participle stays 'finito' — no agreement with avere.

sentences.italian: Non sono sicuro che abbiano capito la domanda.
sentences.literal: I am not sure that they have-subjunctive understood the question.
sentences.natural: I'm not sure they understood the question.

sentences.italian: Mi sembra che siano già partiti.
sentences.literal: It seems to me that they be-subjunctive already left.
sentences.natural: It seems to me they've already left.
sentences.note: 'partire' takes ESSERE → 'siano partiti', NOT 'abbiano partito'. With essere the participle AGREES: 'partiti' (m. pl.).

sentences.italian: Pare che Maria sia arrivata in ritardo.
sentences.literal: It seems that Maria be-subjunctive arrived late.
sentences.note: 'arrivare' takes ESSERE → 'sia arrivata'. The participle agrees with Maria: 'arrivata' (f. sg.), not 'arrivato'.

sentences.italian: Credo che i ragazzi siano andati a casa.
sentences.literal: I believe that the boys be-subjunctive gone to home.
sentences.natural: I think the boys have gone home.
sentences.note: 'andare' takes ESSERE → 'siano andati' (agreeing: m. pl.), NOT 'abbiano andato'.

sentences.italian: Credo che questa funzione non funzioni bene.
sentences.literal: I believe that this function not work-subjunctive well.
sentences.natural: I don't think this feature works well.
sentences.note: Noun vs verb: 'la funzione' = the function/feature (noun); 'funziona / funzioni' = works (verb — subjunctive 'funzioni'). Don't swap them.

sentences.italian: Mi sembra un buon pensiero, ma penso che sia rischioso.
sentences.literal: To me it seems a good thought, but I think that it be-subjunctive risky.
sentences.natural: It seems like a good thought, but I think it's risky.
sentences.note: Noun vs verb: 'il pensiero' = the thought (noun); 'penso' = I think (verb, from pensare). Same root, different words. Also 'sia' NOT 'è' after 'penso che'.

sentences.italian: Penso che venga anche lui alla riunione.
sentences.literal: I think that come-subjunctive also him to-the meeting.
sentences.natural: I think he's coming to the meeting too.
sentences.note: 'anche lui' = him too / he too (NOT 'ancora'). 'ancora' means still/yet — keep them apart. 'venga' is the subjunctive of venire (not 'viene').

sentences.italian: Credo che lei non abbia ancora deciso.
sentences.literal: I believe that she not have-subjunctive yet decided.
sentences.natural: I think she hasn't decided yet.
sentences.note: 'non ancora' = not yet; 'già' = already. Compare 'abbiano già deciso' (already) with this 'non abbia ancora deciso' (not yet). 'decidere' takes avere → 'abbia deciso'.

sentences.italian: Sembra che siano ancora a scuola.
sentences.literal: It seems that they be-subjunctive still at school.
sentences.natural: It seems they're still at school.
sentences.note: 'ancora' here = still. Don't confuse 'ancora' (still/yet) with 'anche' (also/too). 'sembra che' + subjunctive 'siano'.

sentences.literal: It is possible that rain-subjunctive tomorrow.
sentences.note: Don't drop 'che': say 'è possibile CHE piova', never 'è possibile piova'. 'piova' is the subjunctive of piovere.

sentences.italian: Può darsi che abbiano ragione loro.
sentences.literal: It can give-itself that they have-subjunctive reason they.
sentences.natural: Maybe they're the ones who are right.
sentences.note: Keep 'che': 'può darsi CHE abbiano...'. Dropping 'che' is a common mistake. 'abbiano' — avere; 'avere ragione' = to be right.

patternDrill.literal: I believe that he have-subjunctive understood.
I think that they be-subjunctive left.

patternDrill.italian: Mi sembra che tu faccia bene.
patternDrill.literal: It seems to me that you do-subjunctive well.
patternDrill.natural: It seems you're doing the right thing.

patternDrill.italian: È possibile che sia già arrivato.
patternDrill.literal: It is possible that he be-subjunctive already arrived.
I don't believe that it work-subjunctive.
patternDrill.note: 'funzioni' = works (verb, subjunctive) — not 'funziona', and not the noun 'la funzione'.

activeRecall.prompt: I think you need some rest.
activeRecall.answer: Penso che tu abbia bisogno di riposo.

activeRecall.prompt: I think they've already decided everything.
activeRecall.answer: Penso che abbiano già deciso tutto.

activeRecall.prompt: It seems to me they've already left.
activeRecall.answer: Mi sembra che siano già partiti.

activeRecall.prompt: I think he's coming to the meeting too.
activeRecall.answer: Penso che venga anche lui alla riunione.

conversationBuilder.speaker: A
conversationBuilder.italian: Credo che Luca abbia già finito il progetto.
conversationBuilder.natural: I think Luca has already finished the project.

conversationBuilder.speaker: B
conversationBuilder.italian: Non sono sicuro che l'abbia finito. Mi sembra che sia ancora al lavoro.
conversationBuilder.natural: I'm not sure he's finished it. It seems he's still at work.

conversationBuilder.speaker: A
conversationBuilder.italian: Pare che gli altri siano già partiti, però.
conversationBuilder.natural: Apparently the others have already left, though.

conversationBuilder.speaker: B
conversationBuilder.italian: Può darsi. È possibile che venga anche lui più tardi.
conversationBuilder.natural: Maybe. It's possible he'll come too later.

conversationBuilder.speaker: A
conversationBuilder.italian: Sì, penso che sia una buona idea aspettarlo.
conversationBuilder.natural: Yes, I think it's a good idea to wait for him.

grammarExplanation.intro: Once you know the subjunctive exists, the hard part is using it automatically. This lesson drills the five traps that most often break it for B1–B2 learners.
grammarExplanation.explanation: The five traps: (1) slipping back to the indicative after a trigger; (2) the wrong auxiliary in the past subjunctive — avere (abbia/abbiano) vs essere (sia/siano), and with essere the participle agrees; (3) mixing up a noun with a verb (la funzione vs funziona); (4) small confusable words (anche vs ancora, già vs non ancora); (5) dropping the required 'che'.
grammarExplanation.note: Small words: 'anche (lui/lei)' = also/too, NOT 'ancora' = still/yet; 'già' = already, 'non ancora' = not yet. And never drop 'che' after è possibile, può darsi, sembra, pare.

masteryChecklist: Keep the subjunctive after every trigger — never slip back to the indicative (sia not è, faccia not fa, funzioni not funziona).
Pick the right auxiliary in the past subjunctive: avere (abbia/abbiano) vs essere (sia/siano), and make the participle agree with essere.
Tell noun from verb: la funzione vs funziona/funzioni; il pensiero vs penso.
Use anche (lui/lei) vs ancora, and già vs non ancora, correctly.
Never drop 'che' after è possibile, può darsi, sembra, or pare.
